keyPoints: Virtualization allows multiple VMs to share a single physical host's CPU, memory, and I/O resources.
The hypervisor (VMM) is the software layer that manages and allocates these physical resources to virtual machines.
CPU virtualization uses techniques like binary translation and direct execution, with hardware support from Intel VT-x and AMD-V.
Memory virtualization involves shadow page tables or hardware-assisted EPT/NPT to translate physical to machine addresses.
I/O virtualization can be full, para-virtualized, or hardware-assisted (SR-IOV) to manage device access and DMA operations.
commonMistakes: Assuming virtualization overhead is negligible; it can significantly impact performance if not managed properly.
Over-provisioning resources (vCPUs, RAM) leading to resource contention and degraded performance.
Neglecting security considerations like VM escape attacks or inter-VM traffic monitoring.
